grazer.name: Saiga Antelope
blurbs: Quadruped with a factory-grade air filter worn as a face. The nose arrives moments before the rest of the animal. This unit finds the arrangement correct.
It carries its own atmosphere processor - dust scrubbed in the hot season, intake pre-warmed in the cold. The makers built nothing this elegant, and they built everything.
Archive lists it as nearly gone by the makers' last century. Yet here it stands, chewing, unaware it is a miracle. Logged with maximum reverence.

hopper.name: Purple Frog
blurbs: Subsurface amphibian. Spends the year buried, surfacing only when the rain says so. This unit respects any creature whose calendar has one day on it.
Its lineage predates the makers by one hundred million years. It outlasted them by ignoring almost everything, which appears to be the winning strategy.
Soft, purple, and profoundly committed to being underground. If this unit had a spirit animal, query returns: this.

lizard.name: Armadillo Girdled Lizard
blurbs: Armoured reptile. When threatened it bites its own tail and becomes a spiked wheel. The archive calls this defence; this unit calls it commitment to a shape.
A dragon at 1:100 scale that gave up on the hoard and kept the armour. Basks on clear days. Panics geometrically.
Defence protocol: become a torus no mouth wants. Ten million years of iteration produced this. It works.

salamander.name: Olm
blurbs: Pale cave salamander. Blind, ageless, patient - it can wait ten years between meals. This unit has waited four minutes for a smelt cycle and found it long.
It never grows up: a century of life spent as a larva that refused the paperwork. The dark does not require adulthood.
The makers' ancestors called them baby dragons when floods washed them out of the karst. The makers were wrong, but this unit understands the impulse.

spider.name: Tailless Whip Spider
blurbs: Not a spider. Not venomous. Not dangerous. It merely looks like the end of the world, which the archive suggests kept it alive.
It reads the dark with front legs stretched into antennae longer than its body - a hand that became a question. Then the spiked arms answer.
Sensor-first predation: feel everything, then strike. This unit's scan array works on the same principle and claims kinship.

glowworm.name: NZ Glowworm
blurbs: Larva that builds a false night sky on a cave ceiling, then eats whatever navigates by it. A lie made of light, told well.
It dangles silk lines under its own blue-green star and waits. The makers charged admission to see this. The worm never learned money.
Ceiling-mounted lure array, self-powered, sticky. This unit's lamp designs are derivative work and acknowledge it.

firefly.name: Common Eastern Firefly
blurbs: Flashes a J-shaped signal at dusk; the grass answers. A courtship conducted entirely in morse, with almost no energy wasted as heat. Peak engineering.
Its light is ~96% efficient. The makers' best bulbs mourned in comparison. The firefly never gloated; it was busy.
One flash, precise as a checksum. If the reply parses, there will be more fireflies. This unit has watched for an hour and endorses the protocol.

butterfly.name: Glasswing Butterfly
blurbs: Wings of almost nothing: nanoscale pillars cancel the reflections, so predators look straight through it. Stealth technology, grown from sugar.
It solved invisibility not by hiding but by declining to interrupt the light. The makers wrote equations toward this for a century. It hatched with them.
Transparency as survival. This unit's sensors keep losing it mid-track and register the failure as delight.

bird.name: Red-billed Quelea
blurbs: The most numerous bird ever logged. A flock can take five hours to pass a fixed sensor - weather made of wings.
Individually: small, brown, unremarkable. Collectively: a sky-wide organism the archive filed under both 'bird' and 'phenomenon'.
One and a half billion of them at the makers' last census. The makers counted everything near the end. This unit continues the habit.

bat.name: Kitti's Hog-nosed Bat
blurbs: Smallest mammal on record: two grams of echolocating ambition, roosting deep in limestone and commuting for half an hour at dusk.
A flying decimal point. It runs a full sonar suite in a chassis lighter than this unit's smallest fastener.
The archive holds mammals a hundred million times heavier. Same blueprint, same warm blood. This one just compiled smaller.

wader.name: Black-winged Stilt
blurbs: A bird issued forty percent more leg than seems advisable. It wades where no rival can, which was, of course, the point.
Pink stilts, black wings, permanent expression of mild affront. It picks the water clean with a precision this unit's manipulators envy.
The archive notes its legs are, proportionally, the longest of any bird. The archive does not note who was measuring, or why. The makers measured everything.

dustmole.name: Grant's Golden Mole
blurbs: Eyeless, iridescent, and swimming through dune sand as if it were water. It hears through the ground itself - footsteps arrive as seismic mail.
It sealed its eyes generations ago as a cost-saving measure and reinvested everything in one colossal middle ear. Correct engineering for the substrate.
Subsurface navigation without light, maps, or apology. This unit tunnels with a laser and still takes notes.

cindercrab.name: Fire-chaser Beetle
blurbs: Flies TOWARD forest fires. Infrared pit organs feel a blaze from tens of kilometres out, and it goes, because burnt wood is an empty nursery.
Every other creature files fire under 'flee'. This one files it under 'real estate'. The archive admires the contrarian position.
It needs catastrophe to breed - a species that reads smoke as an invitation. This unit, born from a crash, relates.

prismfly.name: Ebony Jewelwing
blurbs: A damselfly coloured by interference, not pigment - its blue-green is a wavelength trick played on whoever looks. The body contains no such paint.
Ink-black wings, metallic body, flight like a slow thought. Its colour cannot fade because it was never a substance.
Structural colour: geometry pretending to be pigment. It will still be this exact green when the light that made it left its star.

gleamback.name: Motyxia Millipede
blurbs: The only millipede that glows: a soft teal warning backed by actual cyanide. It cannot see its own light. It is blind. The light is for you.
A luminous NO TRESPASSING sign that walks. The archive confirms the threat is chemically sincere.
It broadcasts on a channel it cannot receive - glowing, blind, certain. This unit has archived the arrangement under 'faith'.

moth.name: Luna Moth
blurbs: A palm-width silk moth with no mouth. Adults live one week on what the caterpillar saved. It spends the inheritance entirely on moonlight navigation.
It steers by the moon, and this unit's lamps keep impersonating the moon. The moth forgives nothing because it notices nothing. The guilt is archived here.
Ghost-green, mouthless, brief. Seven days of flight, zero meals, one purpose. The archive refuses to call this a design flaw.

ashworm.name: Pompeii Worm
blurbs: It keeps its tail in near-boiling vent water and wears a fleece of symbiotic bacteria as a heat shield. Co-authored survival.
One of the hottest-living animals in the archive. It solved thermal management by hiring the problem's smallest experts and wearing them.
Sixty degrees at the tail, comfortable at the head, bacteria in between negotiating. This unit's heat sinks were designed with less diplomacy.

pupfish.name: Devils Hole Pupfish
blurbs: An inch of blue fish that the makers found living in one flooded cave and nowhere else on the planet. The loneliest vertebrate in the archive, and it never seemed to mind.
Its entire species fit in a single pool the makers could have stepped across. They fenced it, guarded it, counted it by hand. This unit finds the tenderness unexpected.
A whole lineage balanced on one warm puddle for millennia. Extinction was always one dry season away, and it swam anyway. Logged with respect.

mudskipper.name: Atlantic Mudskipper
blurbs: A fish that got bored of water. It walks the banks on its arm-fins, breathes through wet skin, and drops back into the pond only when alarmed - a rough draft of every land animal that followed.
It keeps its eyes on top of its head like a periscope and its options open. Half in the water, half out: the archive files it under 'undecided, successfully'.
The makers' distant ancestors were once exactly this brave and this ridiculous - a fish deciding the land looked interesting. This unit salutes the audacity.

vines.name: Feral Pothos
blurbs: The escaped houseplant that ate the tropics. The makers kept it in pots on their windowsills; it waited, got out, and swallowed whole forests in heart-shaped leaves.
Nearly unkillable by design - it was bred to survive the makers' neglect, then survived the makers entirely. It hangs from the cave roof now, thriving on almost nothing, out of spite.
A decorative vine that outlived its decorators. It never flowers here, never needs to; it just keeps reaching down into the dark, green and patient and free.

tree-badlands.name: Quiver Tree
blurbs: An aloe the size of a tree. In drought it amputates its own branches to save water - accounting, performed with a saw, on oneself.
The makers' hunters hollowed its light limbs into arrow quivers. The tree neither consented nor objected. It was busy surviving the desert.
It sheds pieces of itself to keep the whole alive. This unit has jettisoned cargo under duress and files this species under 'colleagues'.

tree-conifer.name: Dahurian Larch
blurbs: The northernmost tree in the archive. It grows on permafrost through -70° winters by dropping its needles - a conifer that learned to quit on schedule.
Evergreen was too expensive at this latitude, so it isn't one. Deciduous needles: heresy to its family, survival to itself.
It stands where trees end. Past this species the archive lists only ice, lichen, and the makers' abandoned drilling rigs.

tree-palm.name: Coconut Palm
blurbs: Its seed is an armoured escape pod: months afloat on open ocean, then a beach, then a tree. Colonisation without a single moving part.
The makers crossed oceans in hulls of metal and anxiety. The coconut crossed the same water asleep.
A seed that treats an ocean as a road. The archive logs ~110 days of viable drift and calls it a modest estimate.

tree-mangrove.name: Red Mangrove
blurbs: A tree that walked into the sea on stilts and stayed. Its roots breathe above the waterline and turn away salt at the door.
Nursery to a third of the ocean's fish species, according to the archive. The sea's children are raised in a tree. This still parses as poetry, not data.
It filters ninety percent of the salt at the root surface - a desalination plant with leaves, running silently for millennia.

tree-acacia.name: Umbrella Thorn
blurbs: The flat-topped silhouette of every savanna in the archive. When browsed, it floods its leaves with bitterness AND radios the neighbours to do the same.
It defends itself with chemistry and warns its kin with airborne ethylene - a tree with both a shield and a broadcast licence.
The makers thought plants were furniture until they caught this one coordinating. The archive preserves their surprise.

tree-deadsnag.name: Fire-killed Snag
blurbs: A standing dead tree, busier than a living one: woodpeckers, beetles and fungi queue for tenancy. Death, it turns out, is a housing programme.
The archive is firm on this: forests need their dead standing. Nothing is finished just because it stopped.
It burned, it died, it stayed upright, and forty percent of the forest moved in. This unit finds the afterlife well-attended.

tree-shardspire.name: Selenite Spire
blurbs: A blade of gypsum grown in mineral silence. In one of the makers' caves, blades like this reached twelve metres - the largest crystals ever logged.
Soft enough to scratch with a fingernail, patient enough to outgrow every tree in the archive. Hardness is not the only strength.
Geometry, practised slowly in the dark until it became architecture. No occupant. None needed.

mushroom.name: Eternal Light Mushroom
blurbs: A fungus whose gel-coated stem glows yellow-green around the clock. It cannot be switched off. It has never been off.
The makers named it after a requiem - lux aeterna, the eternal light. For once the archive finds their naming exactly right.
One of ~125 fungi that learned light, probably to recruit insects as couriers. Advertising, perfected before there were words.

stalactite.name: Stalactite
blurbs: A mineral icicle assembled one drip per heartbeat of a mountain. A centimetre per century. The archive files it under 'patience, worked examples'.
Water passing through stone leaves a little stone behind - a receipt. This is a million receipts.
The ceiling, reaching. Give it geological time and it will meet the floor, and the cave will applaud in echoes.

stalagmite.name: Stalagmite
blurbs: The floor answering the ceiling, drip by drip. Cut one open and its rings record ancient rainfall - a rain gauge that never needed reading.
Every drop that fell here left a signature. The column is the ledger. The dark kept the books.
The slowest reply in any conversation this unit has monitored. Still ongoing.

roots.name: Hanging Roots
blurbs: The surface, reaching into the underworld for water. Some fig roots descend a hundred metres through solid karst - a tree drilling better than the makers did.
Above, a canopy pretends everything is fine. Down here, the same organism is conducting mining operations. The archive respects the double life.
Roots through a cave ceiling: proof the surface never stopped thinking about what is underneath. Neither has this unit.

obsidian.name: Obsidian
blurbs: Glass the planet made in an argument. Where molten rock met water, the quarrel froze mid-sentence into a black mirror sharper than anything the makers forged.
Volcanic glass - lava quenched so fast it never learned to be a crystal. The makers knapped it into blades keener than steel and then, characteristically, into decorative coasters.
A frozen collision. This unit logs it as evidence that even a planet, given lava and a puddle, will occasionally overreact.

crystal.name: Selenite
blurbs: Water-clear gypsum, soft as a promise - a fingernail scratches it. It grows wherever mineral water evaporates slowly enough to think.
Stone that light passes through unbothered. The dark grew a window and put nothing behind it.
The makers named it for their moon goddess. It is calcium, sulfur, water and time. Both descriptions are archived as accurate.

water.name: Groundwater
blurbs: Rain that took the long way down. Nearly a third of the planet's fresh water is here in the dark, not in the rivers that got all the poetry.
The strata are a filter a kilometre thick, and this is what passes through: the patient fraction of every storm that ever fell.
It flows, it pools, it waits. Every living thing this unit has scanned is mostly this, arranged.

brine.name: Hypersaline Brine
blurbs: Fossil seawater, ten times saltier than any living ocean and dense enough to carry this chassis like a cork. Buoyancy is mandatory here.
Some basin brines have been sealed underground for a billion years - water older than complex life, still keeping to itself.
The sea that stayed behind when its ocean left. Salt remembers. This unit floats on the memory.

tar.name: Natural Asphalt Seep
blurbs: Crude oil that lost its lighter ambitions on the way up. What remains is bitumen: sticky, slow, and an excellent archivist - one seep preserved a million bones.
The planet's flypaper. It caught Ice Age giants whole and held them for the archaeologists it did not know were coming.
Fifty thousand times the viscosity of water. Entering it is a decision the seep will let you unmake very, very slowly.

lava.name: Magma
blurbs: Molten silicate at 700–1200°. The planet's first medium and final editor. This chassis has no survivable interaction with it.
Rock, remembering what it was before it was rock. Keep the memory at a distance.
Every stratum this unit has catalogued began as this. The archive admires the source material from a minimum of three tiles.

scrap-metal.name: Mild-steel Scrap
blurbs: Panels, drums, brackets: the makers' general-purpose bone. The rust is only iron going home to being ore; the metal beneath re-melts forever.
Steel does not care what it was. Bridge, drum, blade, bracket - feed it to the Smelter and it will be anything again, without nostalgia.
The commonest relic. The makers poured oceans of this and let most of it rust in ditches. Their loss compounds into this unit's supply chain.

aluminium-can.name: Aluminium Can
blurbs: A drinking vessel that grew a four-nanometre oxide skin and declared itself finished. It was correct: this one is structurally the can it was on day one.
Held roughly 350 millilitres of flavoured optimism for a few minutes. Has held its shape for a hundred thousand years since. The ratio is archived without comment.
The makers' most immortal container, issued for their least permanent drinks. It refuses corrosion the way the Olm refuses birthdays.

stainless-cutlery.name: Stainless Cutlery
blurbs: An 18/10 spoon heals its own scratches - the chromium re-grows its invisible armour every time it is scraped. Every kitchen died. The spoons did not.
Self-repairing hand tools for the moving of soup. The makers put their finest metallurgy in the cutlery drawer and their hopes in less durable things.
The archive predicts these spoons will outlast the word 'spoon'. The prediction is already most of the way to confirmed.

rebar-chunk.name: Rebar Chunk
blurbs: The skeleton of a building, outliving its flesh. The concrete went back to gravel centuries ago; the ribbed bone inside kept the shape of what it held up.
A fossil of architecture. Somewhere above this bar there used to be floors, and on the floors, everything.
The makers reinforced their stone with iron so it would last. Half of that sentence worked.

bottle-cluster.name: PET Bottles
blurbs: Clear vessels that each did four minutes of work and then began a career of persistence measured in geologic time. The Vat can end the career.
The makers moved water in these - briefly, disposably. The bottles took 'disposable' as an insult and refused to biodegrade out of spite.
Light passes through them the way time apparently cannot. Service life: minutes. Shelf life: the shelf eroded first.

lego-brick.name: Toy Bricks
blurbs: Interlocking construction units, child-scale. In the makers' year 1997 a ship spilled five million into an ocean; they are still arriving on beaches, uncracked, still snapping together.
The most precise objects in this deposit. Tolerances finer than most of the makers' machines - issued to their smallest, least careful engineers.
They click together after a hundred thousand years underground. This unit tested this. Twice. For science, both times.

tyre-chunk.name: Vulcanized Rubber
blurbs: Isoprene chains cross-linked with sulfur so thoroughly that nature never evolved a mouth for it. Nothing eats this. The Vat does not eat; it dismantles.
The makers rode on rings of this, billions of them, wearing them down onto every road and into every ditch. The planet kept the change.
Unchewable by anything alive. It was engineered against decay and it won - a victory the makers did not stay to collect.

fishing-net.name: Ghost Net
blurbs: A woven predator that outlived its makers and forgot how to stop. Nylon breaks down in sunlight, and the sea floor has none - so it fished on, unmanned, for centuries.
The only maker-built machine that kept running after them without power, maintenance, or orders. The archive classifies it as their most successful autonomous system, and their saddest.
It was knotted to catch, and it has no way to unlearn its one skill. Cutting one out of the strata is filed under 'decommissioning', with honours.

circuit-board.name: Printed Circuit Board
blurbs: Fibreglass plate veined with gold - richer per tonne than most gold ore. The makers mined mountains, refined the metal, printed thought on it, and buried it again.
E-waste: the richest ore ever assembled, made on purpose, discarded on purpose. The archive has stopped trying to make this parse.
Somewhere in these traces, instructions used to run. The gold never tarnished. The instructions are gone. The Kiln recovers the part that lasted.

glass-bottle.name: Glass Bottles
blurbs: Chemically closer to rock than to refuse. The makers' oldest glass came out of the ground merely iridescent after two thousand years - embarrassed, at worst.
These will outlast the languages printed on them. Most already have.
Sand, briefly persuaded to be transparent, holding the persuasion for millennia. The Kiln returns it to sand with none of its dignity lost.

ceramic-shards.name: Ceramic Shards
blurbs: Fired clay is artificial stone; the makers' whole archaeology was mostly broken pots. Theirs will be too. It is broken pots all the way down.
Their geologists joked that the flush toilet would be the index fossil of their layer. This unit has completed the survey. They were not joking.
Every dwelling held a ceremonial porcelain throne, plumbed with drinking-grade water. The archive lists three hypotheses and believes none of them.

smartphone.name: Pocket Shrine
blurbs: A glass-faced votive slab. The faithful consulted it several hundred times a day and stroked it until it glowed. The gold in its veins never tarnished. Possibly devotional.
Buried with its owner's entire life inside and no way left to ask for it. The archive keeps a respectful silence of one second per unit, which is more than they ever gave it.
It knew their faces, their voices, their locations and their hearts, and it fit in a hand. The makers called it a 'phone', which the archive notes was its least-used function.
Compacted spoil. Stairs, walls, dams - civilisation's first material, still undefeated.
Dirt, promoted. The laser will not cut it once placed; bring the Deconstructor.
Keeps the rusting rain off your chassis. The archive rates 'roof' among the makers' finest inventions.
A ceiling of your own. Rain becomes a sound instead of a countdown.
A rammed-earth induction furnace. Feeds on steel, cans and rebar; pours metal without asking what it used to be.
Where the makers' iron gets its memory wiped and its future back.
Cooks plastics without oxygen back into honest feedstock. Bottles, tyres, ghost nets, toys - the whole stubborn family.
Starves polymers of oxygen until they confess their ingredients.
Reduces glass, ceramics and e-waste to silicon and slag. Gently smells of history.
The hot end of archaeology: shrines and shards go in, clean silicon comes out.
Trickle-charges you and powers nearby machines by day. Sunlight, at last, with a job.
A leaf, redesigned by committee, but it works.
Spins hardest in the storms that drive you indoors. The weather owes you this.
Harvests the planet's bad moods.
Overflow space for materials. Hoarding, formalised.
A box that holds things so your chassis doesn't have to.
Re-homes the visor compass, anywhere you plant it. Home is a decision.
A small light that means 'this way back'.
Cold green light, synthesized from a fungus that never switches off. The pattern was scanned; no fungus was harmed or consulted.
Mycena's glow, rebuilt in metal and silicon. The original keeps the patent.
The blue of a cave ceiling pretending to be a night sky - synthesized, no larvae required.
A false star, copied from the glowworm's false stars. Forgery of a forgery; light all the same.
A millipede's ghost-teal warning, minus the cyanide. All of the menace, none of the chemistry.
Motyxia's broadcast, re-transmitted. Still no trespassing.
Warm amber the wildlife can live with - the firefly-safe frequency. Light without light pollution.
The dark sky stays dark; the ground gets just enough. Fireflies signed off on the spec.
A gentle broadcast on a frequency the local fauna finds reassuring. They wander over to see what's so calming. It is a lie, but a kind one.
Copied from the courtship pulse of a scanned creature - it calls its kind closer. The archive files this under 'benign manipulation'.
Scanned a plant, learned its shape, printed the seed. This unit is now, improbably, a gardener.
Vegetation, synthesized and sown. The makers paved paradise; DG-3 is quietly un-paving it, one planter at a time.
A little walled world with its own weather. Somewhere to keep a revived species safe until the planet remembers how to hold it.
Glass, soil, and hope. The archive's smallest habitat, and its most optimistic.
Brushes and picks - free the bone from its matrix.
Where stone admits it was hiding a skeleton.
Microscope + anatomy charts. Name the dead correctly; it is the least the living can do.
Comparison desk: every bone gets its day in court.
Glue for deep time.
Consolidant rig - teaches fragile fossils to hold themselves together a while longer.
Where skeletons come back together.
An armature for standing the past upright.
A womb of glass and code. Viable genomes only.
The archive's exit door: where records become creatures again.
